name: Triple Goddesses
shortDescription: The Triple Goddess is a concept found in various forms across Celtic mythology, representing a deity with three aspects or forms, often associated with different stages of life or domains of influence.
description: The concept of the Triple Goddess is a recurring motif in Celtic mythology and neo-paganism. It represents a single deity who manifests in three distinct forms, often associated with the maiden, mother, and crone archetypes. These archetypes symbolize different stages of life, phases of the moon, or aspects of the goddess's power. The maiden represents youth, new beginnings, and potential; the mother embodies fertility, nurturing, and creation; and the crone symbolizes wisdom, endings, and transformation. The Triple Goddess isn't necessarily a single, named deity found uniformly throughout Celtic cultures. Instead, it is a framework for understanding the divine feminine as a multifaceted force. Different goddesses or groups of goddesses can be seen as manifestations of this triple aspect. For example, Brigid, referenced elsewhere, is often seen as a triple goddess of fire, poetry, and healing. The concept of the Triple Goddess highlights the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, as well as the interconnectedness of different aspects of the divine. The Celtic Triquetra symbol is often associated with the Triple Goddess, representing the three aspects as a continuous, interwoven whole. While the explicit articulation of a singular "Triple Goddess" deity is more prevalent in modern paganism, the underlying concepts of threefold divine feminine power are deeply rooted in Celtic mythology and folklore, expressed through various goddesses and their associated attributes.

sources.title: The Pagan Religions of the Ancient British Isles: Their Nature and Legacy
sources.author: Ronald Hutton
